body: Inspect whether the interface keeps users continuously informed about what is happening — particularly after interactions like adding to cart, submitting a form, completing a step, or waiting on data to load. Positive signals include immediate visual feedback after every user action (button state changes, checkmarks, success banners, inline confirmations), progress indicators during any operation that takes longer than a moment, and explicit status messages that describe the current system state in plain language. Negative signals include silent actions where the UI appears unchanged after a click, missing loading states that leave users uncertain whether their action registered, and disappearing or changing UI elements (such as items removed from a wishlist) without any accompanying explanation of why the change occurred. When violations are found, recommend adding action-acknowledgment microcopy or state-change indicators at the point of interaction, ensuring no user action goes visually unacknowledged. Nielsen Norman Group's foundational heuristic #1 states that systems must always keep users informed through appropriate feedback within reasonable time, noting that a lack of information often equates to a lack of control and degrades trust.

benchmark.standard_text: Every user-initiated action receives visible feedback; no silent state changes; system status is always legible without requiring additional interaction

provenance.citation_text: "Visibility of system status" refers to "how well the state of the system is conveyed to its users." The principle emphasizes that "systems should always keep users informed about what is going on, through appropriate feedback within reasonable time." Understanding a system's current state enables users to make informed decisions and take appropriate action. As the article explains, "a lack of information often equates to a lack of control." When users cannot see what's happening, they feel uncertain and cannot assess whether their actions succeeded. Effective feedback can be straightforward—such as button color changes or checkmarks confirming selections. Progress indicators are equally important, as they "reassure the user that a longer wait is normal, and that the system is still working." This immediate feedback prevents users from repeatedly clicking buttons, unsure if the first interaction registered. When systems clearly communicate their state without withholding information, users feel more in control. Failing to explain status changes—like items disappearing from wish lists—"take control away from the user and degrade trust." Instead, explicitly showing which items are unavailable while allowing users to decide their fate fosters confidence in both the interface and the brand.

ai_review.reviewer_persona: neural-heuristic-reviewer v1.0 (top-1% senior CRO consultant; 20yr; multi-vertical D2C/SaaS/marketplace/B2B/lead-gen/fintech/media)
ai_review.why_generated: Drafter targeted Nielsen's Heuristic #1 (Visibility of System Status) — the most-cited NN/g principle and foundation of feedback-loop UX design.
ai_review.how_reviewed: URL is canonical NN/g visibility-system-status article (HTTP 200); citation is verbatim Nielsen prose; broad archetype/page coverage appropriate for universal H1 principle.

dimension_findings.finding: URL https://www.nngroup.com/articles/visibility-system-status/ matches NN/g pattern; HTTP 200 verified
Verbatim NN/g H1 framing; canonical Nielsen heuristic since 1994
6-archetype universal scope appropriate; 7-page-type breadth fits site-wide principle
no conversion-rate predictions; phrasing is principle-level/recommendation-form
qualitative standard_text paraphrases Nielsen normative statement faithfully
visible-feedback-per-action is concrete + universally-applicable consultant guidance
